headline: RON95 Quota Cut April 1: 90% 'Unaffected' Figure Has No Published Basis
context: The monthly subsidised RON95 quota under BUDI95 dropped from 300 to 200 litres on 1 April 2026. Government says 90% of users consume under 200 litres; the underlying methodology was not published. Subsidised price stays at RM1.99/litre; above-quota purchases pay market rate.

cards.big: RON95 stays at RM1.99 for the first 200 litres. The government says 90% of users won't notice.
cards.sub: The 90% figure was stated by MOF without a published methodology or sample period. The quota dropped from 300 to 200 litres on 1 April 2026.

cards.big: Above-quota RON95 sold near market rate from 1 April. The week of 16-22 April listed RM3.87 versus the subsidised RM1.99.
cards.sub: The gap is roughly 95% on a per-litre basis. Heavy-use drivers, gig riders and rural commuters cross the 200-litre threshold most often.

cards.big: Indonesia's earlier subsidy reforms ran alongside direct cash assistance such as BLT BBM. Malaysia retained STR and SARA but did not announce a dedicated BUDI95 transitional transfer.
cards.sub: Each country's quota design, transfer and timing differ; the comparison is directional.

cards.big: Rural and East Malaysia have thinner public-transport networks than the Klang Valley. Daily commuter alternatives are limited; coverage varies by district.
cards.sub: The quota cut took effect five days after the Aidilfitri festive maximum price scheme ended on 28 March.

cards.big: The question is not whether 90% are unaffected. It is whether a 'targeted' reform can claim that figure without publishing how it was computed.
Reform without published targeting methodology, a transitional package, or an alternatives plan is reform on assertion. The arithmetic should be on the table before the price is.
